question: How do Platform Channels work? Implement a MethodChannel example.
answer:
Platform Channels allow Flutter (Dart) to communicate with native code (Android/Kotlin, iOS/Swift).

Three channel types:
• MethodChannel — request/response. Flutter calls native method, awaits result. Best for: battery level, camera, sensors.
• EventChannel — continuous stream FROM native. Best for: accelerometer, location updates.
• BasicMessageChannel — raw message passing with a custom codec.

Both sides must use the SAME channel name string. Messages are serialized via StandardMessageCodec (supports: null, bool, int, double, String, Uint8List, List, Map).

question: How do you write a Flutter plugin from scratch?
answer:
A Flutter plugin is a Dart package that wraps native platform code (Android + iOS) using platform channels. Publishing a plugin makes it reusable across projects.

Plugin structure:
• lib/ — Dart API (what app developers use)
• android/ — Kotlin implementation
• ios/ — Swift implementation
• test/ — unit and integration tests
• example/ — runnable demo app

Create with: flutter create --template=plugin --platforms=android,ios my_plugin

The Dart side defines the interface and delegates to native via MethodChannel.

question: What is EventChannel and when do you use it instead of MethodChannel?
answer:
EventChannel is for a continuous, push-based stream of events from native to Dart. Where MethodChannel is request/response, EventChannel is publish/subscribe.

When it fits:
• Sensor data — accelerometer, gyroscope, GPS
• Bluetooth or BLE notifications
• Hardware buttons, NFC reads, system battery state changes
• Foreground service progress (download, audio playback position)

Mechanics:
• Dart calls `receiveBroadcastStream()` — native receives `onListen(arguments, EventSink)`
• Native pushes events via `events.success(payload)`; errors via `events.error(...)`
• When the Dart subscription is cancelled, native receives `onCancel(arguments)` — release resources here
• Multiple Dart listeners share one native subscription on the broadcast stream — design for fan-out

When not to: a one-shot value (use `MethodChannel.invokeMethod`); high-frequency hot data over 1kHz (use FFI + ring buffer instead — channel serialization will dominate cost).

question: What is Pigeon and why use it over hand-written MethodChannels?
answer:
Pigeon is the official Flutter code-gen tool for type-safe platform channels. You declare the interface once in Dart, and Pigeon emits Dart, Kotlin, and Swift bindings that talk to each other through the existing channel mechanism.

Why it beats raw MethodChannel:
• Type safety on BOTH sides — no untyped Map<String, dynamic>, no string method names sprinkled across the codebase
• Refactor support — rename a method and the analyzer fails on every caller in Dart, Kotlin, and Swift simultaneously
• Custom data classes are codegen'd as @HostApi/@FlutterApi DTOs — equality, copyWith, JSON-style codec for free
• Bidirectional — `@HostApi` is Dart→native, `@FlutterApi` is native→Dart callbacks
• Async by default — generated methods return Future on Dart side, suspend/Combine-style on native

When to use raw channels:
• Quick spike or one-off prototype
• Existing plugin you don't own

In anything beyond a couple of methods, Pigeon is the right default.

question: How does the threading model work for platform channels, and how do you avoid blocking the UI?
answer:
Channels look async to Dart, but the native side runs on the platform thread (main thread on iOS, main thread on Android by default). If your Kotlin/Swift handler does CPU or IO work synchronously, the platform thread blocks — and on Android that means jank for the entire app.

Threading rules:
• Dart: every `invokeMethod` is async — returns a Future. The send is non-blocking; the wait is.
• Android: handlers run on the platform's main thread. Move heavy work off — `kotlinx.coroutines` with Dispatchers.IO, or AsyncTask-style executor pools — and call `result.success()` from any thread.
• iOS: handlers also run on the main queue. Push work to a background queue with DispatchQueue, and call back on the main queue (Flutter's BinaryMessenger requires it on iOS).
• Result must be called exactly once. Calling twice is an exception. If your handler returns synchronously without calling result, Dart hangs forever.

Further: heavy native callbacks fanning into Dart with EventChannel can saturate the platform thread too. Throttle on the native side or batch events.

question: How should you handle errors across a platform channel?
answer:
Errors are first-class on channels — both sides have an explicit way to signal failure. Wire them up correctly and your Dart code can branch on a typed PlatformException; skip them and exceptions become Map-shaped soup.

Dart side:
• Native success → Future completes with the value
• Native `result.error(code, message, details)` → Future throws `PlatformException(code, message, details)`
• Native `result.notImplemented()` → throws `MissingPluginException` (e.g. running on a platform where the plugin isn't registered)
• A timeout is on you — channel calls have no built-in deadline

Native side:
• Always call result exactly once. Catch your exceptions and convert them to result.error.
• Use stable string codes (NETWORK_TIMEOUT, PERMISSION_DENIED) — not localized messages. Localize in Dart.
• Include enough detail in `details` for diagnostics, but never put PII or tokens there.

Design tip: model expected failures (no permission, no network) as result.error with a known code; reserve thrown exceptions for bugs.

question: When should you choose dart:ffi over a MethodChannel?
answer:
MethodChannel and FFI both cross the Dart/native boundary, but they solve different problems.

MethodChannel:
• Talks to Kotlin/Swift/ObjC — anything written for the platform's Java VM or Objective-C runtime
• Each call serializes through the StandardCodec and crosses to the platform thread — typical latency on the order of milliseconds
• Best for: SDKs you must integrate (Firebase, Maps, Camera) and platform UIs (permission dialogs, system UI)

dart:ffi:
• Talks to plain C ABI — pure native libraries (.so/.dylib/.dll)
• Direct call into C — sub-microsecond latency, no serialization, no thread hop
• Memory is on you — calloc/free, raw pointers, no GC across the boundary
• Best for: high-throughput pure logic in C/C++/Rust (codecs, image processing, hashing, ML)

Decision rule:
• Need to talk to Activity / View / UIWindow / system permissions → MethodChannel (or Pigeon over MethodChannel)
• Need to call a CPU-heavy library at high frequency → FFI
• Need both? Many production apps do — FFI for the hot path, MethodChannel for the platform-API parts

Note: many official plugins (sqlite3, image, native_video_player) already use FFI under the hood — you don't need to reach for it directly unless you ship your own native code.

question: How do you call platform code from a background isolate?
answer:
Platform channels are bound to a specific BinaryMessenger, which by default lives on the root isolate. Background isolates (Isolate.spawn / Isolate.run / compute) cannot just instantiate a MethodChannel — they have no messenger and would silently fail.

Flutter 3.7+ ships BackgroundIsolateBinaryMessenger which lets a background isolate piggy-back on the root isolate's messenger:
• On the root isolate: capture `RootIsolateToken.instance!`
• Send it to the background isolate as part of its initial message
• In the background isolate, call `BackgroundIsolateBinaryMessenger.ensureInitialized(token)` BEFORE creating any MethodChannel
• After that, MethodChannel calls work as normal — the message hops to root, then to platform, then back

Why you might still avoid this:
• It still serializes through the root isolate. If the root is busy, the channel call queues.
• For pure CPU work, move the work to the isolate but NOT the platform calls — fetch raw bytes on root, hand them to the isolate, return processed bytes.
• For background fetch / WorkManager scenarios, the isolate is created BY native code; it must register a Dart entry point and call ensureInitialized before its first platform call.

question: What is BasicMessageChannel and when does it beat MethodChannel?
answer:
BasicMessageChannel is the lowest-level channel: it sends arbitrary messages with no method-call envelope and a configurable codec. MethodChannel is just a convention on top of it — "a Map with a method name and arguments".

When it's the right tool:
• Symmetric, peer-to-peer messaging — both sides send and receive on equal footing (no "caller" vs "handler")
• High-volume binary payloads where StandardMessageCodec is wasteful (use BinaryCodec to skip Map wrapping)
• Custom serialization — you have a JSON or Protobuf representation already and don't want to repack into the standard codec
• Bridging non-method-call protocols (raw events, command pipelines)

Codec choices:
• StandardMessageCodec — default; supports primitives + List + Map
• StringCodec — UTF-8 strings only (lightweight)
• JSONMessageCodec — passes through dart:convert
• BinaryCodec — passes raw ByteData; you serialize yourself (Protobuf, FlatBuffers)

When NOT to: vast majority of apps. MethodChannel is more discoverable; Pigeon is even better for typed interfaces. Reach for BasicMessageChannel only when you have a specific reason.
